title: Brick Like Me

jokes.text: It's not selling out. It's co-branding. Co-branding!
jokes.setup: Homer is having a nightmare and talking in his sleep
jokes.punchline: His unconscious anxiety is about the corporate concept of 'co-branding' rather than anything personal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer anxiety - worried about corporate buzzwords even in his sleep. Sets up the episode's theme about commercialism vs authenticity.

jokes.text: Oh, honey, the best part of every day is waking up to your smiling face... Just like the best day of my life was when you gave me your hand in marriage. I'd like it back, please.
jokes.setup: Homer delivers romantic sentiment about Marge giving him her hand in marriage
jokes.punchline: Reveals he means literally her detached Lego hand
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that works on multiple levels - romantic language subverted by Lego literalism, perfectly structured with genuine sentiment leading to absurd physical reality.

jokes.text: Hey! Drop it, you stupid dog! That's one of my wife's pieces! She needs it to reciprocate high fives!
jokes.setup: Dog has taken Marge's Lego hand
jokes.punchline: Homer's specific concern about her inability to reciprocate high fives
jokes.explanation: Extends the Lego hand gag with Homer's characteristically specific and absurd priorities. 'Reciprocate high fives' is wonderfully clinical language for such a silly concern.

jokes.text: That's okay, Homie. I'll just grab another. Mmm... Oh... I haven't worn this one since New Year's.
jokes.setup: Marge casually dismisses losing her hand
jokes.punchline: She treats interchangeable Lego hands like accessories with sentimental value
jokes.explanation: Solid extension of the Lego world logic with Marge's characteristic acceptance and organization. The nostalgia for a hand is absurd but perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Your father's wearing a tie. Oh, that's what's different! That's the one and only thing.
jokes.setup: Homer asks if something seems weird today, family notices something different
jokes.punchline: They identify Homer's tie as the ONLY difference while being Lego people
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - the characters are oblivious to their fundamental transformation while fixating on a minor detail. The emphatic 'one and only thing' sells the absurdity.

jokes.text: Aw, they're so cute when they're Duplo.
jokes.setup: Maggie is playing with food
jokes.punchline: Marge uses 'Duplo' (larger Lego blocks for toddlers) instead of a normal descriptor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant single-word joke that perfectly adapts familiar parenting language to the Lego world. 'Duplo' replacing 'little' is economical and clever world-building.

jokes.text: I was distracted driving.
jokes.setup: Homer crashes into someone and apologizes
jokes.punchline: He matter-of-factly admits to distracted driving as his 'awesome excuse'
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic obliviousness to social norms. Treating dangerous driving as a valid excuse is perfectly stupid in his voice.

jokes.text: Oh, don't worry about it. Good thing we don't feel pain.
jokes.setup: Homer crashes into someone
jokes.punchline: The victim casually mentions they don't feel pain, revealing Lego world physics
jokes.explanation: Efficient world-building humor that establishes Lego physics while making light of what should be trauma. Dark but delivered matter-of-factly.

jokes.text: Hey! These are the monkey's legs!
jokes.setup: Aftermath of collision with scattered Lego pieces
jokes.punchline: Someone identifies specific animal legs among the debris
jokes.explanation: Solid Lego world logic - body parts are interchangeable pieces that can be lost and found. Minor but consistent with the episode's premise.

jokes.text: Come back! I'm a clown! I can't afford to look ridiculous!
jokes.setup: Krusty is missing body parts from the collision
jokes.punchline: A clown worrying about looking ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Perfect character irony - a clown's professional reputation depends on controlled ridiculousness, so random ridiculousness threatens his livelihood. Economical and sharp.

jokes.text: It can't spray-- it's been de-sacked. Ew! The gypsy skunk-seller lied!
jokes.setup: Bart brings a skunk to school, claims it can't spray
jokes.punchline: It sprays, leading to the revelation about a 'gypsy skunk-seller'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from simple prank to elaborate backstory. 'Gypsy skunk-seller' is wonderfully specific and absurd, plus 'de-sacked' is a great made-up word.

jokes.text: Stop it! If you pull out those bricks, the whole school could collapse! But there's a skunk in there. Scottish steak!
jokes.setup: Skunk chase leads to structural concerns about the school
jokes.punchline: Willie's random outburst of 'Scottish steak!' during the crisis
jokes.explanation: Willie's nonsensical exclamation is perfectly in character - Scottish and food-related for no logical reason. The structural collapse concern adds Lego world stakes.

jokes.text: This says: ages 12 and up. Age guidelines are conservative, and everyone knows it.
jokes.setup: Bart is assigned to rebuild the school with Lego instructions
jokes.punchline: His dismissive attitude toward age recommendations
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Bart's rebellious confidence. The idea that age guidelines are 'conservative' shows his sophisticated rationalization of rule-breaking.

jokes.text: Haw-haw! Thank you, Nelson.
jokes.setup: Skinner asks Nelson for 'words of support' for Bart
jokes.punchline: Nelson just does his signature laugh, and Skinner politely thanks him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Nelson's 'haw-haw' IS his form of support/mockery, and Skinner's polite acknowledgment treats it as a formal contribution. Economical and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Ah. Always good to meet a fellow AMFOP. Huh? Adult Male Fan of Princesses. It's for my daughter. Yes, yes, it's always for the daughter.
jokes.setup: Homer buys princess toy, Comic Book Guy assumes he's a fellow enthusiast
jokes.punchline: CBG's knowing dismissal of the 'for my daughter' excuse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy moment - creating acronyms for niche fandoms and assuming everyone shares his obsessions. 'AMFOP' is a great fake acronym.

jokes.text: Well, there's no dad on the box. I don't want to get in trouble.
jokes.setup: Lisa asks if Homer wants to build the toy with her
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation of packaging imagery as rules
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic - treating marketing imagery as legal requirements. His fear of 'getting in trouble' for violating box art is perfectly stupid.

jokes.text: We played... and it wasn't boring. But I'm letting you in on a secret. When parents play with their kids, they don't like it. And I'm no different.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes he enjoyed playing with Lisa
jokes.punchline: His honest confession about parental play-acting
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest parenting humor that rings universally true. Homer's surprised self-discovery and willingness to share uncomfortable truths is both funny and touching.

jokes.text: Suddenly I can't breathe. Every fiber of my being screams out for a nap. And if someone handed me an issue of The New Yorker, I would read the fiction. I swear to God I would.
jokes.setup: Homer describing typical parent-child play experience
jokes.punchline: Escalating desperation culminating in the ultimate parental boredom threat
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation from physical to existential desperation. The New Yorker fiction threat is perfect - ultimate parental boredom expressed through highbrow cultural surrender. Highly quotable and true.

jokes.text: But there are millions of parents in the world. Surely some of them like playing with... Nope, not even one.
jokes.setup: Homer considers if maybe some parents enjoy child's play
jokes.punchline: Immediately and definitively shuts down the possibility
jokes.explanation: Great interruption of his own thought process. The absolutism of 'not even one' out of millions is perfectly unreasonable and funny.

jokes.text: Candy Land.
jokes.setup: Homer describes 'the most inhumane torture ever devised by man'
jokes.punchline: It's the innocent children's board game Candy Land
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/payoff with maximum economy. Building up dramatic tension about 'inhumane torture' only to reveal the most innocent game possible. Every parent knows this pain.

jokes.text: My body was squishy, and my hands looked like snakes made of meat!
jokes.setup: Homer describes his weird dream to Marge
jokes.punchline: His horrifying description of normal human anatomy from a Lego perspective
jokes.explanation: Brilliant perspective flip - normal human features described as grotesque from a Lego viewpoint. 'Snakes made of meat' is a wonderfully disturbing way to describe fingers.

jokes.text: Oh, Homie, it was probably just a mini-stroke. You're just saying that to make me feel better.
jokes.setup: Homer describes his disturbing visions
jokes.punchline: Marge casually suggests brain damage, Homer finds this comforting
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamics - Marge's matter-of-fact medical concern and Homer's gratitude for any explanation, even neurological damage. Dark but sweet.

jokes.text: I only don't ask because being rejected gets old.
jokes.setup: Homer says he always needs intimacy from Marge
jokes.punchline: Admits he doesn't ask to avoid repeated rejection
jokes.explanation: Honest relationship humor that many couples recognize. Homer's matter-of-fact acceptance of martial rejection patterns is both sad and funny.

jokes.text: Marge? Did you replace our regular mirror with a magical mirror from a mystical salesman at a weird store that if we went back to find it, it wouldn't be there anymore?
jokes.setup: Homer sees his real reflection and suspects the mirror
jokes.punchline: His overly specific description of a fantasy trope
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-humor about fantasy story conventions. Homer's immediate jump to the most elaborate magical explanation shows both his pop culture awareness and rejection of simple solutions.

jokes.text: Just a one-time thing. Oh, brick me!
jokes.setup: Homer tries to reassure himself about the mirror incident
jokes.punchline: Uses 'brick me' as a Lego-world swear word
jokes.explanation: Nice world-building detail - Lego people would have Lego-appropriate profanity. 'Brick me' as an exclamation is creative and fits the character's stress.

jokes.text: I, sir, am in the Advent calendar! December 18, final week!
jokes.setup: Conflict between Homer and another Lego figure
jokes.punchline: The figure asserts his legitimacy through Advent calendar placement
jokes.explanation: Funny assertion of status through holiday merchandise placement. The specificity of 'December 18, final week' makes it more absurd and believable.

jokes.text: What the...? This isn't beer. Beer is plastic circles. How can I drink... this?
jokes.setup: Homer tries to drink at Moe's
jokes.punchline: Confusion about liquid beer vs. Lego representation
jokes.explanation: Good Lego world logic - liquids would be represented by solid plastic pieces. Homer's confusion about how to consume non-solid 'beer' is perfectly reasonable from his perspective.

jokes.text: I put in a rock-climbing wall, all the classrooms are skate parks, we got zip-line stairwells, Terminator gym teachers, your office is now a haunted forest-- extra ghosts-- and, if you can believe it, two tetherball poles.
jokes.setup: Bart explains how he redesigned the school
jokes.punchline: Escalating list of ridiculous improvements ending with mundane tetherball
jokes.explanation: Great escalation that captures Bart's fantasy of the perfect school. 'Extra ghosts' is a nice detail, and ending with tetherball as the unbelievable capstone is perfect anticlimax.

jokes.text: How will children learn if they don't feel like they're in kid jail?
jokes.setup: Skinner objects to Bart's fun school design
jokes.punchline: Admits schools are intentionally prison-like
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner moment - accidentally revealing the authoritarian philosophy behind traditional education. 'Kid jail' is both accurate and funny from an authority figure.

jokes.text: Yo soy language lab.
jokes.setup: Bart mentions using Ralph in the school reconstruction
jokes.punchline: Ralph declares himself to be the language lab in Spanish
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph non-sequitur that's both nonsensical and logically consistent with being used as a building component. The Spanish adds to the randomness.

jokes.text: Then the Great Constructor scissored open bag one and dumped out the universe.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy reads from Lego scripture
jokes.punchline: Genesis told from the perspective of toy construction
jokes.explanation: Clever religious parody adapted to toy world cosmology

jokes.text: acrylonitrile butadiene styrene, or in the common tongue, 'plastic.'
jokes.setup: Religious creation story about the universe being made of eternal, unchanging material
jokes.punchline: The scientific chemical name for ABS plastic treated as holy language
jokes.explanation: Brilliant world-building humor - treating the technical name for plastic as religious scripture. The 'common tongue' framing makes it feel ancient and sacred.

jokes.text: You mean like decals? Well, the Orthodox don't use them, but we're a Reform congregation.
jokes.setup: Homer suggests there's more to the world than plastic
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy interprets this as Lego customization options and references religious denominations
jokes.explanation: Excellent parallel between religious denominational differences and Lego purist vs. customization debates. Shows deep understanding of both religious and toy collecting cultures.

jokes.text: Oh, Homer, a place like that could only exist in some kind of magic rock song.
jokes.setup: Homer describes a world where things don't snap together
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy dismisses it as fantasy requiring 'magic rock song'
jokes.explanation: 'Magic rock song' is a wonderfully bizarre way to describe impossibility. The specificity makes it funnier than just saying 'fantasy.'

jokes.text: I think they have a name, but I can't put my finger on it.
jokes.setup: Homer describes his hands looking like weird wiggly things
jokes.punchline: Can't 'put his finger on' what fingers are called
jokes.explanation: Perfect pun that works on multiple levels - literal inability to identify fingers while using the finger-based idiom. Economical and clever wordplay.

jokes.text: He's a freak! Take him apart and lose the pieces under the couch!
jokes.setup: Crowd sees Homer's human-like hands
jokes.punchline: Threat to disassemble him like a broken toy
jokes.explanation: Dark but funny Lego-world justice - ultimate punishment is being taken apart and having pieces lost. 'Under the couch' makes it more specific and relatable.

jokes.text: Bad news, people. Our religion is not true. Sorry about that. Really sorry.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy looks up 'fingers' and discovers truth about their world
jokes.punchline: Casually announces the falseness of their entire belief system
jokes.explanation: Masterful understatement - treating the collapse of fundamental religious truth as a minor inconvenience. The repeated apologies make it funnier.

jokes.text: City Hall, the weird-smelling bank, Rehab World... Krustyburger, Krustyburger Express, the Krustyburger where the governor got stabbed...
jokes.setup: Lisa describes their mini-Springfield model
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of gubernatorial stabbing location as a landmark
jokes.explanation: Good Springfield humor - treating political violence as a tourist landmark. The escalation from normal businesses to 'Rehab World' to stabbing location captures the town's dysfunction.

jokes.text: Hey, Lenny, remember those two surfers we were gonna fight? Well, you're on your own. But you're the one who sat on their fish tacos.
jokes.setup: Homer cancels plans with Lenny to spend time with Lisa
jokes.punchline: Random backstory about surfer conflict over fish taco sitting
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully random conflict with perfect Homer logic - abandoning a friend but ensuring the original transgression (taco-sitting) is remembered. Absurd specificity makes it funny.

jokes.text: I don't want to brag, but... it really brews.
jokes.setup: Homer made a Duff brewery for their model
jokes.punchline: His pride in making functional alcohol production in a toy
jokes.explanation: Good Homer priority - making toy alcohol actually work

jokes.text: I wonder what the one swear word will be. What if it's... Oh, I've never heard that one. My grampa said it at Thanksgiving.
jokes.setup: Milhouse wondering about PG-13 rating swear word allowance
jokes.punchline: His grandfather's Thanksgiving profanity as reference point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse moment - his innocent curiosity about profanity and unexpected family context. Grandpa's holiday swearing is a relatable detail.

jokes.text: I assume Lisa told you about the pretend tiny town she's building with her overweight father.
jokes.setup: Homer continues trying to impress Lisa's friends
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating description that makes the hobby sound even less cool
jokes.explanation: Extends the cringe with Homer's tone-deaf self-awareness. Calling himself 'overweight' while trying to sound cool perfectly captures his social ineptitude.

jokes.text: I have three dads.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains about having dads
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of having three fathers
jokes.explanation: Modern family structure humor delivered matter-of-factly. Short and economical, though not particularly original in contemporary comedy.

jokes.text: One of them wears deodorant. I don't know which one.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains why the older girls are appealing
jokes.punchline: Deodorant use as mysterious marker of sophistication
jokes.explanation: Perfect child perspective on adolescent sophistication. The mystery of which one uses deodorant captures how young kids view teenage maturity markers.

jokes.text: It's a really complicated time in a girl's life from age eight to... actually, all the rest of the way.
jokes.setup: Marge explains Lisa's growing up to Homer
jokes.punchline: Realizes complexity continues indefinitely
jokes.explanation: Great parenting humor that rings universally true. The self-correction from specific age range to 'forever' captures the reality of raising girls (or anyone).

jokes.text: This is for the tacos! Ow! Tacos, brah! Ow!
jokes.setup: The surfers Homer and Lenny were supposed to fight arrive
jokes.punchline: They're still angry about the fish taco incident
jokes.explanation: Solid callback that pays off the earlier random surfer conflict setup. The commitment to taco-based revenge is absurd but consistent.

jokes.text: She's a little... bustier than I remember. My work on that front is never done.
jokes.setup: Homer comments on his Lego figure of the movie character
jokes.punchline: Admits to anatomically enhancing the toy figure
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer horniness combined with his obsessive attention to detail. 'My work is never done' treats it as professional dedication, which is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: She's a little... bustier than I remember.
jokes.setup: Homer describes the book character he built
jokes.punchline: Admitting he enhanced her physical attributes
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer inappropriate focus delivered sheepishly

jokes.text: One of the main questions I have about that is, why? The real Homer fears losing his daughter's love, so he invented this toy world where nothing will ever change.
jokes.setup: CBG explains the fantasy world revelation
jokes.punchline: His clinical analysis of the emotional psychology behind the premise
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor with CBG serving as genre-savvy exposition. His matter-of-fact analysis of psychological motivations fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: I'm afraid so. I'm trapped in a fantasy forever! Kiss my flat plastic butt, reality.
jokes.setup: CBG confirms Homer could be trapped forever
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate embrace of the situation with Lego-specific insult to reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - instant acceptance of the absurd situation. 'Flat plastic butt' is a great Lego-world-specific insult that acknowledges their physical form.

jokes.text: Marge, who would give up eating steak in the Matrix to go slurp goo in Zion?
jokes.setup: Marge questions living in a pretend paradise
jokes.punchline: Homer's Matrix reference about choosing pleasant illusion over harsh reality
jokes.explanation: Smart pop culture reference that perfectly captures the philosophical dilemma. Homer choosing sensory pleasure over truth is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, hook me up with some more of that imaginary nectar.
jokes.setup: Homer plays tea party with Lisa
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic engagement with pretend tea
jokes.explanation: Good character development showing Homer's genuine engagement. 'Imaginary nectar' treats pretend tea with mock sophistication that shows he's actually enjoying himself.

jokes.text: Good Lord, I'll never experience the ultimate reward for a life well lived-- the gentle slumber of death.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes the downsides of eternal toy world existence
jokes.punchline: His poetic description of death as life's ultimate reward
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark humor that's both philosophical and perfectly Homer. 'Gentle slumber of death' as the 'ultimate reward' is beautifully poetic for such a dark concept.

jokes.text: Lego Marge, you're just as cool as Real Marge. Who?! Nobody, nobody.
jokes.setup: Homer compliments Lego Marge
jokes.punchline: His panicked cover-up when she questions 'Real Marge'
jokes.explanation: Good slip-up humor with Homer's terrible cover-up skills. The repeated 'nobody' makes his panic more obvious and funny.

jokes.text: But I miss burning my mouth on pizza, and David Blaine stunts where he could really die.
jokes.setup: Homer explains why he wants to return to reality
jokes.punchline: Missing both mundane pain and vicarious death anxiety
jokes.explanation: Good observation about missing both small and large real-world stakes

jokes.text: As an adult who surrounds himself with child's toys, I represent the part of your psyche that prefers this artificial world.
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy reveals himself as the antagonist
jokes.punchline: His self-aware analysis of his own psychology and role in the fantasy
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor that uses CBG's actual character traits as plot elements. His clinical self-analysis while being the villain is perfectly in character and clever writing.

jokes.text: That's... that's mis-set-genation.
jokes.setup: Bart's robot is made from multiple different toy sets
jokes.punchline: CBG's pun on 'miscegenation' using Lego set terminology
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pun that perfectly captures Comic Book Guy's pedantic nature and collector mentality. Using racial terminology for toy mixing is both offensive and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I'm a creative but undisciplined builder!
jokes.setup: Bart controls his mixed-set robot in battle
jokes.punchline: His self-assessment as explanation for his building style
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart self-awareness that frames his rule-breaking as artistic vision. 'Creative but undisciplined' captures both his positive and negative traits.

jokes.text: Oh, Meat Lisa, it's you.
jokes.setup: Homer wakes up from the Lego dream
jokes.punchline: Still thinking in Lego terms, calls real Lisa 'Meat Lisa'
jokes.explanation: Perfect transition joke that shows Homer's perspective shift. 'Meat Lisa' is both disgusting and endearing in context, showing lingering Lego worldview.

jokes.text: Oh, Meat Lisa, it's you.
jokes.setup: Homer wakes up from his Lego dream
jokes.punchline: Still thinking in Lego terms about human anatomy
jokes.explanation: Good transition moment maintaining the perspective shift

jokes.text: Mmm, isn't that kind of the plot of the...? No. No, it's not. It's a new plot.
jokes.setup: Lisa starts to point out the dream parallels a movie
jokes.punchline: Homer's defensive insistence on originality
jokes.explanation: Great meta-humor acknowledging the episode's obvious Lego Movie parallels while having Homer defensively deny it. Self-aware but not apologetic.

jokes.text: When do they get to killing the children? Oh, I just wanted to see kids fight to the death, is all.
jokes.setup: Homer watches Hunger Games movie and gets bored with romance
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact desire for child violence as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that captures Homer's simple entertainment preferences. His casual dismissal of romance in favor of violence is perfectly in character and slightly disturbing.

tmdb_overview: Homer wakes up in a Springfield where everyone and everything is made out of Legos, and he must figure his way out before he gets stuck in the plastic world forever.
